metadata.title: Engineers Are Learning the Wrong Things (The System Thinking Gap)

beats.narration_text: Vanakkam — this is Liam, in for Bear. Based on the essay 'We're Teaching Engineers to Do the Wrong Things' by Aditi Shinde, for NortheasternISE / Humanitarians AI. What are engineers actually being trained to do? The floor has dropped. AI does what the curriculum teaches faster and cheaper. What remains irreducibly human is systems thinking: deciding what problems are worth solving, which stakeholders' needs take priority, what values we're optimizing for. The Boeing 737 MAX was not a code failure.

props.segment: Engineers Are Learning the Wrong Things (The System Thinking Gap)
props.command: What are engineers actually being trained to do?
props.runningText: reading engineering education and AI displacement analysis…
output: The curriculum floor dropped: AI does calculation, retrieval, pattern-matching faster.
What remained: deciding what problems to solve, which values to optimize for.
Boeing 737 MAX: engineers designed the component without understanding the system.

beats.narration_text: The execution floor of engineering work is falling. Calculation: AI does it faster. Retrieval: AI does it faster. Documentation: AI does it faster. Pattern-matching on known problem classes: AI does it faster. These are tasks the curriculum was built around — because for a century, human ability to perform them quickly was economically valuable. The machines that arrived are superhuman at answering the kinds of questions engineers were hired to answer. What the curriculum did not build, because it did not need to, is what remains: the judgment that decides which questions are worth asking. That floor has not dropped. It is empty.

manim.description: Two-level floor diagram. Top floor: 'technical execution' (calculation, retrieval, documentation, pattern-matching) — floor drops to machine level, labeled 'AI does this faster'. Bottom floor: 'systems judgment' (problem selection, values, stakeholder priorities) — floor stays, but appears empty. Empty floor highlighted.
manim.sparkLine: The floor dropped. Below it: empty.

beats.narration_text: Two lists. What AI supersedes: calculation and retrieval, documentation, pattern-matching on known classes, boilerplate generation, code syntax, literature lookup. What remains irreducibly human: deciding what problem is worth solving, determining which stakeholders' needs take priority when they conflict, understanding what values the system is being asked to optimize for, recognizing when the system is solving the wrong problem, knowing when to stop and discard working code. The Boeing 737 Max engineers were excellent at the first list. They designed a component without understanding how it interacted with the full system. The failure was in the second list — systems thinking at scale.

animated_deck.description: Two diverging lists from same origin. Left: 'AI supersedes' — calculation, retrieval, documentation, pattern-matching, boilerplate, syntax, lookup. Right: 'Irreducibly human' — problem selection, stakeholder priority, values optimization, wrong-problem recognition, knowing when to stop. Lists grow, diverge.
animated_deck.sparkLine: Two lists. One shrinking. One not.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Two lists. What AI supersedes: calculation and retrieval, documentation, pattern-matching on known classes, boilerplate generation, code syntax, literature look

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The Boeing 737 MAX was not a code failure — engineers designed a component without understanding the larger system. AI does what the curriculum teaches faster and cheaper: calculation, retrieval, documentation, pattern-matching. What remains irreducibly human is judgment: deciding what problems are worth solving, which values to optimize for. The floor that dropped: execution. The floor that is empty: judgment, because the curriculum never built it.

props.artifactTitle: Engineers Are Learning the Wrong Things (The System Thinking Gap)
artifactLines: Boeing 737 MAX: component designed without system understanding — not a code failure.
AI supersedes the curriculum: calculation, retrieval, documentation, pattern-matching.
Floor that dropped: execution. Floor that is empty: judgment.
Irreducibly human: deciding what problems to solve, which values to optimize for.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt — read it with me. 'Map the curriculum of your engineering program against the irreducibly human list. Which courses build judgment? Which build execution? What's the ratio?' That ratio is your curriculum's bet about what will be valuable in ten years. Count the execution courses and the judgment courses. The gap between them is the system thinking gap — the empty floor. Run this mapping in your own Claude session. Liam, in for Bear.
